q: The two leading causes of vision impairment worldwide are:
w: Uncorrected refractive error and cataract lead globally, and both are largely avoidable.

q: What fraction of global vision impairment is avoidable (preventable or curable)?
w: Over 80% of vision impairment is preventable or curable, mainly refractive error and cataract.

q: A disease constantly present in a community at a low, steady level is:
w: Endemic = constant low-level presence. Epidemic = outbreak above expected; pandemic = epidemic across regions.

q: A statistic counting only NEW cases over a period measures:
w: Incidence = new cases over time; prevalence = all existing cases at a point.
k: Incidence = new cases; prevalence = all cases.

q: Community vision screening and health education to prevent disease before onset is which level of prevention?
w: Primary prevention acts before onset; secondary = early detection/reversal; tertiary = limit established disability.

q: The leading cause of bilateral blindness in the Philippines is:
w: Cataract (~33%) is the leading cause, followed by refractive error and glaucoma.

q: The World Health Organization was established on:
w: WHO was founded April 7, 1948 (World Health Day), governed through the World Health Assembly.

q: Winslow's definition of public health centers on three verbs:
w: Public health = preventing disease, prolonging life, and promoting health through organized community effort.

q: Low-vision rehabilitation to limit disability in an established eye condition is which level of prevention?
w: Tertiary prevention limits disability once disease is established; secondary catches/reverses it early.

q: The Philippine DOH program integrating eye care into local public health is the:
w: The CEHP delivers avoidable-blindness care through the barangay-to-tertiary service delivery network.

q: Myopia is corrected with which lens?
w: Myopia (image in front of retina) is corrected with a minus lens; hyperopia with plus.

q: The far point of a −3.00 D myope is:
o: 33 cm in front of the eye
w: Far point distance = 1/3.00 m = 33 cm in front of the eye for a −3.00 D myope.

q: Hofstetter's MINIMUM amplitude of accommodation formula is:
w: Minimum = 15 − 0.25(age); average = 18.5 − 0.30(age); maximum = 25 − 0.40(age).

q: Total hyperopia equals:
w: Total hyperopia = latent (masked by ciliary tone) + manifest (revealed on routine refraction).

q: Pseudomyopia is:
w: Pseudomyopia is a real tonic accommodative spasm that makes an emmetrope appear myopic and resolves with cycloplegia.
k: Pseudomyopia = accommodative spasm; clears with cycloplegia.

q: With-the-rule (WTR) corneal astigmatism is:
o: Steep at 180°, more common with age
Steep at 90°, more common in youth
w: WTR is steep in the vertical (90°) meridian, common in youth; ATR (steep at 180°) becomes common with age.
k: WTR steep at 90°, youth; ATR steep 180°, age.

q: At a 67 cm retinoscopy working distance, you subtract how much from the gross finding?
w: 67 cm WD = 1.50 D, so subtract 1.50 D from the gross to get the net Rx. (50 cm WD = subtract 2.00 D.)

q: In retinoscopy, 'against' motion is neutralized with which lens?
w: Against motion = add minus; with motion = add plus.

q: A young hyperopic child with esotropia that resolves with full plus glasses has:
w: Uncorrected hyperopia drives accommodation and convergence; full cycloplegic plus removes the drive.
k: Hyperopia + eso at near = accommodative esotropia.

q: The far point of a hyperope is:
w: A hyperope's far point is virtual (behind the eye); the emmetrope's is at infinity.
k: Hyperope far point = virtual, behind eye.

q: Which best describes ABSOLUTE hyperopia?
w: Absolute hyperopia exceeds the available accommodation, so it cannot be cleared even at distance.

q: Average presbyopia onset is around what age?
w: Presbyopia symptoms typically begin around age 45 as the lens nucleus hardens.

q: Anisometropia carrying the highest amblyopia risk is a difference greater than:
w: Differences greater than ~3.00 D (especially hyperopic) carry high amblyopia risk; ~6 D is the practical spectacle limit.

q: Compound myopic astigmatism in minus-cylinder form is written as:
w: Both principal meridians are myopic, so minus-cyl form uses a minus sphere with a minus cylinder.
k: Compound myopic astig (minus cyl) = minus sph + minus cyl.

q: Astigmatism is best corrected with:
w: Astigmatism needs a cylindrical (or sphero-cylindrical) lens to correct the two different meridional powers.

q: Against motion in all meridians at 67 cm working distance indicates myopia of more than:
w: Against motion at 67 cm (1.50 D WD) means myopia greater than 1.50 D.

q: If pinhole acuity improves the vision, the cause is most likely:
w: Pinhole improvement implies refractive (correctable) cause; no improvement suggests organic disease.

q: In the unilateral cover test, covering the right eye makes the uncovered left eye move inward, then it drifts back out when uncovered. This is:
w: The left eye was deviating outward (exo) under cover and recovered on uncovering, a latent deviation = exophoria.

q: A temporally displaced corneal light reflex indicates which deviation?
w: A temporal reflex corresponds to an eso deviation; a nasal reflex corresponds to exo.
k: Temporal reflex = eso; nasal reflex = exo.

q: The pupillary light reflex pathway leaves the visual pathway at the:
w: Pupillomotor fibers branch off at the pretectum, which is why cortically blind patients keep light reflexes.
k: Pupil reflex exits at pretectum (not LGN).

q: A normal near point of convergence (penlight) break is about:
w: Normal NPC break ~7 cm (±4); >10 cm suggests convergence insufficiency, <5 cm convergence excess.

q: The alternating cover test:
o: Tests one eye at a time only
Shows total deviation, not its type
w: The alternating cover dissociates fully and shows total deviation; the unilateral cover test distinguishes phoria vs tropia.
k: Alternating cover = total deviation, not phoria/tropia.

q: A relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) is detected with the:
w: The swinging flashlight test reveals an RAPD: the affected pupil paradoxically dilates when light swings to it.

q: Standard far visual acuity testing distance is:
w: Far VA is tested at 6 m (20 ft); near VA at ~40 cm (16 inches).
k: Far VA at 6 m; near at 40 cm.

q: The near reflex triad consists of:
w: Looking at near triggers pupil constriction, accommodation, and convergence together.

q: A patient with a frontal headache and eyestrain most classically has:
w: Frontal headaches map to hyperopia/convergence excess; temporal to astigmatism; occipital to presbyopia/CI.

q: The average adult interpupillary distance is about:
w: Mean adult PD ~64 mm (range 54–68 mm). Near PD = DPD × WD/(WD + 27).

q: Binocular visual acuity, compared with the better monocular eye, should be:
w: Binocular VA should equal or exceed the better eye; worse binocular VA suggests a binocular problem.

q: On Hirschberg testing, a reflex at the pupillary margin corresponds to about:
w: Reflex at the pupil margin ≈ 15° ≈ 30Δ of deviation.

q: A pinhole that does NOT improve reduced acuity suggests:
w: No pinhole improvement points to organic disease (macula, optic nerve) rather than refractive error.

q: The near point of convergence is tested:
w: NPC is binocular: both eyes track a target inward until convergence breaks.

q: A direct light reflex refers to:
w: The direct reflex is ipsilateral constriction; the consensual (indirect) reflex is contralateral.

q: Direct ophthalmoscopy produces what kind of image?
w: Direct = virtual, erect, ~15x, narrow ~6.5° field. Indirect = real, inverted, 2–5x, wide ~40° field.

q: Increasing the power of the indirect condensing lens (e.g., +20 D vs +14 D) gives:
w: Higher condensing power = lower magnification but wider field of view.
k: Higher condensing lens = wider field, less mag.

q: Which is best for examining the peripheral retina?
w: Indirect ophthalmoscopy gives the wide field needed for periphery; direct is best for fine disc detail.
k: Indirect = peripheral retina; direct = disc detail.

q: On the Van Herick test, which grades are unsafe to dilate?
w: Grade 0–1 (narrow/closed angle) should not be dilated; grades 3–4 are safe.

q: Javal's rule estimates which value?
w: Javal's rule: At = 1.25(Ac) + (−0.50 cyl × 90°), estimating total astigmatism including the internal component.

q: The cobalt blue slit-lamp filter is used to:
w: Cobalt blue excites fluorescein for corneal staining; the red-free (green) filter is for blood vessels.

q: In opacity parallax, an ANTERIOR opacity moves which way relative to the ophthalmoscope?
w: Anterior opacities move opposite to the scope; posterior opacities move in the same direction.
k: Anterior opacity moves opposite; posterior same.

q: The keratometer measures:
o: Total corneal power including the back surface
w: The keratometer reads only the anterior corneal surface using a simplified index, hence Javal's rule for total astigmatism.

q: The internal (lenticular) astigmatism component used in Javal's rule is approximately:
w: Internal astigmatism is a roughly constant −0.50 cyl × 90° (ATR direction) that partly offsets corneal WTR.

q: The red-free (green) slit-lamp filter is best for:
o: Viewing vessels and nerve fiber layer
w: Red-free light makes vessels and the nerve fiber layer appear dark for better contrast.

q: The normal retinal vein-to-artery (V:A) caliber ratio is:
w: Normal V:A is 3:2; veins are slightly larger than arteries. Narrowed arteries occur in hypertension.

q: The slit lamp set at a 60° angle is used for:
w: At 60°, the slit lamp performs the Van Herick angle estimate and specular reflection of the endothelium.

q: Average normal keratometry readings fall around:
w: Normal central K is ~40–47 D, averaging about 43–44 D.

q: Indirect ophthalmoscopy produces an image that is:
w: The indirect image is real and inverted (2–5x), with a wide field, opposite to the direct's virtual erect image.

q: For WTR corneal astigmatism, the keratometer minus axis is near:
w: WTR has its minus (flat) axis near 180° (steep vertical); ATR has minus axis near 90°.

q: A Van Herick shadow about one-quarter of the optic section width corresponds to which action?
o: Do not dilate under any setting
Van Herick does not guide dilation
w: A ~1:4 ratio is a moderately narrow angle (about grade 2): dilate cautiously and watch for closure.

q: On the astigmatic dial, the darkest (clearest black) line indicates the:
w: The darkest line marks the power meridian; the minus-cyl axis is 90° away from the darkest line.

q: For every −0.50 D of cylinder you add during the astigmatic dial, you add how much sphere?
w: Add +0.25 D sphere per −0.50 D cyl to keep the circle of least confusion on the retina.

q: On the duochrome test, if the GREEN side is clearer, the patient is:
w: Green clearer = focal point in front of retina = over-minused; reduce minus or add plus. (Red clearer = add minus.)
k: Green clearer → reduce minus / add plus.

q: During JCC, the patient prefers Position 1 (red dots parallel to the tentative axis). This means:
w: Preferring the deliberately over-corrected Position 1 means the cylinder is still under-powered, so add minus cyl.
k: Prefer JCC Position 1 → add minus cyl.

q: The ideal amount of fog before the astigmatic dial is:
w: Fog +0.50 to +0.75 D above best sphere to blur to ~20/30–20/40, relaxing accommodation.

q: During the JCC axis check, the handle (axis of the flip cross) is held:
w: For the axis check the handle is parallel to the tentative cylinder axis; for the power check a dot is parallel to the axis.
k: JCC axis check: handle parallel to axis.

q: The rotation (clock-dial) method for refining cylinder is used when the cylinder power is:
w: The rotation/astigmatic-dial method is used for cyl > 0.75 D; JCC is used for ≤ 0.75 D.

q: In binocular balancing, the re-fog (added plus) is applied to:
w: Add +0.50 to +0.75 D to BOTH eyes so accommodation is equally relaxed during balancing.

q: The prism dissociation setup for binocular balance is:
w: 3Δ base-down OD and 3Δ base-up OS vertically separates the two images for comparison.
k: Prism dissociation: 3Δ BD OD / 3Δ BU OS.

q: During binocular balance, adding +0.25 D to the clearer eye REVERSES the preference instead of equalizing. You should:
o: Add plus to the other eye
w: A reversal means you passed the neutral point; allow the dominant eye to keep the slight advantage.
k: Reversal → dominant eye keeps better VA.

q: PRA (positive relative accommodation, OEP #20) is measured by adding:
w: PRA adds minus until first sustained blur; NRA (#21) adds plus until blur.
k: PRA = add minus; NRA = add plus.

q: The duochrome test is invalid in patients with:
w: Red-green color blindness makes the duochrome comparison meaningless.

q: The endpoint of the OEP #7 subjective refraction is:
w: #7 ends at MPMVA and becomes the reference lens for all subsequent OEP tests.

q: On the duochrome test, if the RED side is clearer, you should:
w: Red clearer = focal point behind retina = under-minused; add minus (or reduce plus).

q: OEP test #3 uses which correction, and #8 uses which?
w: #3 is taken with the habitual Rx (before subjective); #8 uses the Test #7 result.

q: Preferring JCC Position 2 (white dots parallel to the axis) indicates:
w: Position 2 is the under-corrected state; preferring it means the cylinder is over-powered, so reduce minus.
k: Prefer JCC Position 2 → reduce minus cyl.

q: The correct order of managing a binocular vision disorder is:
w: Always start by correcting the refractive error, then prism, then vision therapy, then surgery.
k: Order: correct RE → prism → VT → surgery.

q: Exophoria requiring prism is corrected with which base direction?
w: Exophoria → base-in prism; esophoria → base-out; hyperphoria → base-down before the higher eye.

q: The Sheard's-criterion prism amount is calculated as:
w: Prism = (2 × phoria − compensating recovery) ÷ 3.

q: The first management step for accommodative esotropia is:
w: Full plus removes the accommodative drive causing the esotropia; surgery and patching are not first-line.

q: In conventional occlusion therapy for amblyopia, you patch the:
w: Cover the good eye to force use of the amblyopic eye. Atropine penalization is likewise instilled in the good eye.

q: Strabismus surgery for a patient with amblyopia:
w: Surgery corrects motor alignment only; amblyopia (acuity) still needs patching/penalization/VT.
k: Surgery aligns; it does not treat amblyopia.

q: Pleoptics is specifically indicated for amblyopia with:
w: Pleoptics targets eccentric fixation and gives inferior results to patching for standard amblyopia.

q: The MOST sensitive period for amblyopia development and treatment is:
w: The critical period is most sensitive 0–2 years, treatable to ~7–8, with some response up to 12–18.

q: Vertical (hyper) phoria prism is placed base-___ before the ___ eye:
w: Base-down prism before the higher eye (or base-up before the lower eye) corrects a hyperdeviation.
k: Hyperphoria: base down before the higher eye.

q: Atropine penalization for amblyopia works by:
o: Blurring the good eye at near
w: Atropine in the sound eye blurs its near vision, forcing the amblyopic eye to work at near.

q: Over-patching the sound eye risks:
w: Excessive patching can make the previously good eye amblyopic, so VA in both eyes is checked each visit.

q: Sudden-onset distance esophoria (possible divergence insufficiency) must be differentiated from:
w: DI is comitant; CN6 palsy is incomitant (worse in the paretic field). Rule out CN6 palsy before prescribing BO prism.

q: For an 8Δ exophoria with a base-in recovery of 10Δ, Sheard's prism is:
w: Prism = (2×8 − 10)/3 = 6/3 = 2Δ; exophoria takes base-in.

q: The first management step for anisometropic amblyopia is:
w: Give the full refractive correction first so the amblyopic eye gets the clearest image; then add patching if needed.

q: Before starting vision therapy for a binocular disorder, you must first:
w: Glasses come first; VT on an uncorrected Rx trains against the wrong baseline.

q: The Parks 3-step test is used for:
w: Parks 3-step (8→4→2→1) isolates a paretic cyclovertical muscle in vertical deviations.

q: On the Brock string, a normal response is:
o: An X at the fixated bead
w: Normal viewing shows an X at the fixated bead; an absent X side indicates suppression. The string is both diagnostic and therapeutic.
k: Brock string normal = X at fixated bead.

q: The CAM (visual stimulator) treatment is performed:
w: CAM is a monocular procedure; the dominant eye is occluded. Sessions ~7 min, once weekly.

q: Lens flippers for accommodative infacility typically use:
w: ±2.00 D flippers train accommodative facility; the adult target is ~12 cycles per minute.

q: For moderate amblyopia, part-time patching (2–6 hrs/day) is:
w: PEDIG trials showed 2–6 hrs/day is comparable to full-time patching for moderate amblyopia.

q: Sheard's and Percival's criteria are used to decide when to prescribe:
w: These criteria determine prism prescription; VT is chosen to build capacity rather than just compensate.

q: A typical office-based VT sequence for convergence insufficiency progresses as:
w: CI therapy builds convergence: pencil push-ups, then Brock string, aperture rule, then prism/vectogram flippers.
k: CI VT: push-ups → Brock → aperture → vectogram.

q: The normal near positive fusional convergence (PFC) finding is approximately:
w: Near PFC norm ≈ Blur 17 / Break 21 / Recovery 11 Δ.

q: The Hart chart trains primarily:
w: The Hart chart alternates near and distance targets, training accommodative facility and saccades.

q: When should patching be discontinued?
o: After exactly two weeks of wear
As soon as any improvement is seen
When VA equalizes or stops improving
w: Stopping at first improvement causes regression; continue until VA equalizes or plateaus across cycles.
k: Stop patching at equalization/plateau, not first gain.

q: The Brock string, when the X pattern is absent on one side, indicates:
w: A missing leg of the X means that eye's input is being suppressed.

q: The number of NEW cases arising over a period of time is the:
w: Incidence counts new cases over time; prevalence counts all existing cases at a point in time. Chronic disease inflates prevalence without changing incidence.

q: A disease constantly present in a population at low frequency is described as:
w: Endemic means habitually present at low, predictable frequency. An epidemic is a sharp rise above the expected baseline.

q: The two leading global causes of visual impairment are:
w: Uncorrected refractive error and cataract lead globally, which matters because both are correctable. Over 80% of visual impairment worldwide is avoidable.

q: The leading cause of blindness in the Philippines is:
w: Cataract is the leading Philippine cause of blindness, addressed through the national Cataract-free zone and CEHP programs.
k: PH leading cause of blindness = cataract.

q: Winslow's classic definition of public health centers on the verbs:
w: Winslow framed public health as preventing disease, prolonging life, and promoting health through organized community effort.

q: The World Health Organization was founded on:
w: WHO was founded 7 April 1948, now marked annually as World Health Day.

q: Hofstetter's formula for the MINIMUM amplitude of accommodation is:
w: Minimum = 15 − 0.25(age), average = 18.5 − 0.30(age), maximum = 25 − 0.40(age). The minimum line is what flags a clinically low amplitude.

q: Hofstetter's formula for the AVERAGE amplitude of accommodation is:
w: The average line, 18.5 − 0.30(age), predicts the expected amplitude for a patient's age; the minimum line is 15 − 0.25(age).

q: The far point of a −3.00 D myope lies:
o: 33 cm in front of the eye
w: The far point sits at 1/3.00 = 0.33 m in front of the eye. A hyperope's far point is virtual, behind the eye; an emmetrope's is at infinity.

q: The far point of a hyperopic eye is:
o: Real, in front of the eye
w: Only converging light focuses on an uncorrected hyperopic retina, so the conjugate far point is virtual and sits behind the eye.
k: Hyperope far point = virtual, behind the eye.

q: By Donders' table, the expected amplitude of accommodation at age 40 is about:
w: Donders gives about 10.00 D at 20 and 4.50 D at 40. Once the amplitude can no longer sustain the near demand, presbyopia declares itself, on average around 45.

q: With retinoscopy at a 67 cm working distance, you correct the gross finding by:
w: The working distance adds its own dioptric value, so at 67 cm you subtract 1.50 D from the gross to reach the net refraction.

q: 'With' motion on retinoscopy means you should:
w: With motion means the far point is behind the retinoscope, so plus is added until neutrality. Against motion calls for minus.
k: With = add plus; against = add minus.

q: With-the-rule astigmatism has its steep power meridian at:
w: WTR has the steep (more powerful) meridian vertically, at 60–120°, so the correcting minus cylinder axis sits at or near 180°.

q: The standard illumination for visual acuity testing is:
w: Charts are standardized at about 20 foot-candles. Under-illumination falsely depresses acuity, especially in cataract.

q: An acuity of 20/200 expressed in decimal notation is:
w: 20/200 = 6/60 = 0.1 decimal. For comparison 20/20 = 6/6 = 1.0.

q: Visual acuity that does NOT improve with a pinhole suggests:
w: The pinhole removes blur from refractive error. If acuity stays poor, the deficit is organic (media opacity, retinal or optic nerve disease) rather than refractive.

q: The mean adult interpupillary distance is about:
w: Mean adult PD is about 64 mm, ranging 54–68 mm. Near PD is calculated as NPD = DPD × WD ÷ (WD + 27).

q: The pupillary light reflex fibers leave the visual pathway at the:
w: Pupillary fibers branch off at the pretectal nucleus in the midbrain, before the LGB. This is why a cortical lesion blinds without touching the light reflex.
k: Pupil fibers leave at the pretectum, not the LGB.

q: A relative afferent pupillary defect is also known as a:
w: An RAPD, or Marcus Gunn pupil, signals an afferent conduction defect and is elicited with the swinging flashlight test.

q: The near reflex triad consists of convergence, accommodation and:
w: The near triad is convergence, accommodation and miosis, all driven together by CN3.

q: On Hirschberg testing, a corneal reflex displaced temporally indicates:
w: The reflex shifts opposite to the turn: a temporally displaced reflex means the eye is turned in, an esodeviation.

q: The image formed by direct ophthalmoscopy is:
w: Direct ophthalmoscopy gives a virtual, erect image at about 15× over a narrow 6.5° field, which is why it excels at disc detail. Indirect gives a real, inverted image at 2–5× over 40°.
k: Direct = virtual, erect, 15×; indirect = real, inverted.

q: Increasing the power of the condensing lens in indirect ophthalmoscopy gives:
o: A narrower field and more magnification
No change in field or magnification
A wider field and less magnification
A wider field and more magnification
w: Field and magnification trade off: a 30 D lens gives high magnification over a small field, a 20 D lens a wider field with less magnification.
k: Higher condensing power = wider field, less mag.

q: Which Van Herick grades permit safe dilation?
w: Grades 3 and 4 mean an open angle and safe dilation; grades 0–1 mean a dangerously narrow angle and dilation is withheld.

q: The average normal keratometry reading is about:
w: Normal K readings span roughly 40–47 D, averaging 43–44 D, matching the cornea's ~43 D contribution.

q: In Javal's rule, the internal (lenticular) astigmatism constant is taken as:
w: Javal's rule is At = 1.25(Ac) + (−0.50 cyl × 90°). The fixed internal component runs in the against-the-rule direction and partly offsets corneal WTR astigmatism.

q: On parallax testing, an opacity that moves in the SAME direction as the scope lies:
w: Posterior opacities travel with the scope; anterior opacities move against it. The pupil plane is the pivot.

q: Against-the-rule corneal astigmatism has its correcting minus cylinder axis at or near:
w: ATR has the steep meridian horizontal, so the minus cylinder axis sits near 90°. WTR puts that axis near 180°.

q: The target acuity a patient should reach when properly fogged is:
w: Fogging to 20/30–20/40 relaxes accommodation without over-blurring. That corresponds to about +0.50 to +0.75 D above best sphere.

q: On the astigmatic dial, the darkest line corresponds to the:
w: The darkest line marks the power meridian. The minus cylinder axis lies 90° away from it, which is the rule-of-thumb the dial exists to deliver.
k: Darkest line = power meridian; axis is 90° away.

q: When adding cylinder, you compensate the sphere by:
w: Adding minus cylinder pulls the circle of least confusion forward, so half the cylinder power is added back as plus sphere to keep it on the retina.

q: On JCC axis refinement, when the patient prefers Position 1 you should:
w: Preferring Position 1, with the red dots parallel to the tentative axis, means the cylinder is under-powered, so more minus cylinder is added. Position 2 means it is over-powered.

q: On the duochrome test, a patient who says the RED side is clearer is:
w: Red clearer means the focus sits in front of the retina's long-wavelength focus, so the patient is under-minused: add minus or reduce plus. Green clearer is the reverse.
k: Red clearer = add minus; green clearer = add plus.

q: The standard prism dissociation setup for vertical balance is:
w: Three prism dioptres base-down right and base-up left splits the target vertically so each eye is judged separately without fusion.
k: Prism dissociation = 3Δ BD OD / 3Δ BU OS.

q: In binocular balance, the re-fog applied to both eyes is:
w: Both eyes are re-fogged +0.50 to +0.75 D so the balance is judged with accommodation relaxed and equal.
k: Balance re-fog = +0.50 to +0.75 D both eyes.

q: For the PRA (OEP #20), you add:
w: Positive relative accommodation is measured by adding minus until sustained blur, testing accommodation against fixed convergence. NRA adds plus instead.
k: PRA = minus to blur; NRA = plus to blur.

q: Relieving prism for an exophoria is prescribed:
w: Prism is placed base toward the deviation's correction: base-in for exophoria, base-out for esophoria, base-down before the higher eye in hyperphoria.

q: For a hyperphoria, the base-down prism is placed before the:
w: Base-down before the higher eye pushes its image back down toward fusion.
k: Hyperphoria: BD before the higher eye.

q: Sheard's formula for the prism to prescribe is:
w: Sheard's prism = (2 × phoria − recovery) ÷ 3, applied when the compensating recovery falls below twice the phoria.

q: Percival's criterion asks that the phoria fall within:
o: The middle third of the vergence range
The outer third of the range
w: Percival's criterion ignores the phoria's size and asks only that the demand sit in the comfortable middle third of the total vergence range.
k: Percival: phoria in the middle third.

q: Borish's threshold for clinically significant anisometropia is:
w: Two dioptres or more is the classic threshold. At a hyperopic difference of 3.5 D or more, amblyopia is essentially universal without correction.

q: The first management step in DEPRIVATION amblyopia is to:
w: Deprivation amblyopia needs the obstruction cleared first, since no optical or occlusion therapy works through a dense cataract.
k: Deprivation amblyopia: remove the opacity first.

q: Pleoptics is indicated specifically for:
w: Pleoptics dazzles the peripheral retina to rehabilitate foveal fixation in eccentric fixation. It is largely historical and gives results inferior to conventional patching.

q: The CAM stimulator protocol runs:
w: CAM uses 7-minute sessions once a week over several weeks, with the dominant eye occluded. Evidence for it is weak against standard patching.

q: Vision therapy differs from prism in that therapy:
w: Therapy trains near the vergence system's limit and expands the zone of clear single binocular vision. Prism only compensates, leaving the range unchanged.
k: VT expands ZCSBV; prism only compensates.

q: An absent X on the Brock string indicates:
w: The X crossing at the fixated bead proves both eyes are contributing. If one leg disappears, that eye is being suppressed.
k: No X on Brock string = suppression.

q: The normal NRA finding is:
w: Negative relative accommodation normally reaches about +2.00 D. A reduced NRA points to accommodative insufficiency or convergence insufficiency.

q: Standard lens flipper training uses which power, aiming at what adult rate?
w: ±2.00 D flippers targeting 12 cycles per minute in adults treat accommodative infacility.

q: The Hart chart trains accommodation together with:
w: Alternating between near and distance Hart charts exercises accommodative facility and saccadic accuracy at the same time.

q: Antisuppression therapy normally progresses:
w: Suppression is broken peripherally first, where it is weakest, then centrally, then built up through synoptophore Grades I, II and III.
k: Antisuppression: peripheral → central → I → II → III.

q: PEDIG evidence on patching dose for moderate amblyopia showed that:
w: PEDIG found part-time patching (2–6 hours daily) matches full-time patching for moderate amblyopia, with better compliance.

q: A disease is constantly present in a community at a low, steady frequency. This pattern is:
w: Constant low-level presence in a locality. An epidemic is an outbreak above expected levels; a pandemic is an epidemic spread across regions.
k: Constant low-level presence in a locality.

q: Conducting community vision screening and health education to stop disease before it starts is which level of prevention?
w: Primary prevention acts before onset. Secondary is early detection and reversal; tertiary limits established disability (e.g., low-vision rehab).

q: In the Philippines, the leading cause of bilateral blindness is:
w: (about a third of cases), followed by errors of refraction, then glaucoma.
k: (about a third of cases), followed by errors of refraction, then glaucoma.

q: A statistic counts only the new cases of glaucoma diagnosed in a region during one year. This measures:
w: New cases over a defined period. Prevalence would count all existing cases at a point in time.
k: New cases over a defined period.

q: In the Philippines, the leading cause of bilateral low vision (as opposed to blindness) is:
w: (~43%), with cataract second (~34%). The order flips from blindness, where cataract leads.

q: Which of the following does WHO list as a condition that does not typically cause vision impairment?
w: Along with blepharitis, chalazion/stye, conjunctivitis, dry eye, and subconjunctival hemorrhage. The other three are on the list of conditions that can cause VI.
k: Along with blepharitis, chalazion/stye, conjunctivitis, dry eye, and subconjunctival hemorrhage.

q: A 7-year-old is referred for an eye examination. The parents report that the child squints to see the blackboard but reads comfortably at near. Retinoscopy reveals AGAINST motion in all meridians at 67 cm working distance. Which lens is most likely to neutralize the reflex?
w: Against motion at 67cm (1.50D WD) means the patient is myopic by more than 1.50D. If the gross finding is −1.50D (the value that produces neutral), the net finding = −1.50 + (−1.50) = −3.00D. Against motion requires MINUS lenses for neutralization. A and B are wrong because plus lenses are for with motion. C is the gross finding, not the net finding.
k: Against motion at 67cm (1.50D WD) means the patient is myopic by more than 1.50D.

q: A 45-year-old emmetrope complains that she needs to hold newspapers further away to read comfortably. Using Hofstetter's average formula, what is her expected accommodative amplitude?
w: Average AOA = 18.5 − 0.30 × 45 = 18.5 − 13.5 = 5.00 D. This is the average expected amplitude. Minimum = 15 − 0.25 × 45 = 15 − 11.25 = 3.75D. Maximum = 25 − 0.40 × 45 = 25 − 18 = 7.00D. The question asks for the average Hofstetter formula result, so the answer is 5.00D.

q: During a preliminary examination, the examiner covers the patient's right eye. The uncovered left eye is observed to move inward (nasally). Upon removing the occluder from the right eye, the left eye moves back outward. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
w: The uncovered left eye moved INWARD (nasal) when the right was covered. This means the left eye wanted to drift outward (exo direction) when fusion was released by covering. When the cover was removed, the left eye moved back out to its normal position — consistent with a PHORIA (latent deviation controlled by fusion). The inward movement of the left eye when uncovered means the left eye was EXO under cover. Since it corrected on uncovering and there was no movement on the unilateral cover test, this is a phoria, not a tropia.
k: The uncovered left eye moved INWARD (nasal) when the right was covered.

q: A patient's VA is OD 20/60, OS 20/20. Pinhole placed over the right eye improves VA to 20/30 but not to 20/20. The most likely explanation is:
o: The right eye has an uncorrected refractive error only
The pinhole result is invalid — a 20/60 eye should always reach 20/20 through pinhole
The right eye has both an uncorrected refractive error AND an organic component
The right eye has a purely organic pathology
w: Pinhole improvement indicates a refractive component (since aberrations were reduced and VA improved from 20/60 to 20/30). However, 20/20 was not reached, indicating the improvement is incomplete — an organic component (amblyopia, early cataract, macular pathology) is also limiting the final VA. Pure refractive error alone would be expected to reach 20/20 (or close to it) through pinhole. A = wrong because some improvement occurred. B = wrong because the improvement was incomplete.
k: Pinhole improvement indicates a refractive component (since aberrations were reduced and VA improved from…

q: During slit lamp examination of a patient being evaluated for dilation, the Van Herick shadow width appears equal to about one-quarter of the corneal optic section width. Which of the following actions is most appropriate?
o: Measure IOP before deciding, then dilate freely if normal
Proceed with dilation — the angle is wide open
Dilate with caution and monitor for angle closure
Do not dilate — the angle is closed
w: A shadow width equal to 1/4 of the corneal optic section width corresponds to Van Herick Grade 2 (ratio 1:4). This indicates a narrow anterior chamber angle — not closed, but at risk. Dilation is not absolutely contraindicated but must be done with caution, the patient should be monitored, and the IOP checked after dilation. Grade 0–1 = absolute contraindication. Grades 3–4 = safe to dilate. Grade 2 = proceed with caution.
k: A shadow width equal to 1/4 of the corneal optic section width corresponds to Van Herick Grade 2 (ratio 1:4).

q: Which of the following is NOT part of the slit lamp's mechanical system?
w: The light source belongs to the ILLUMINATION system (source, condenser, slit, filters). The mechanical system moves and steadies the instrument and patient: the joystick, the sliding/motorized base and table, and the positioning frame (chin and forehead rest). Watch for this "which is NOT mechanical" phrasing — the light source is the trap answer.
k: The light source belongs to the ILLUMINATION system (source, condenser, slit, filters).

q: You need to survey a patient's ocular adnexa, then examine the cornea, then check the anterior chamber for cells and flare. Which magnification progression is most appropriate?
w: Adnexa is a wide low-power survey (6x), the cornea is a middle-layer view (16x), and cells/flare in the anterior chamber need the highest magnification in a fully dark room (25–40x). Remember: higher magnification always means a smaller field of view, so you start low to find the target and zoom in for detail. The Van Herick angle estimate sits in the middle at 16x with the arm at 60°.
k: Adnexa is a wide low-power survey (6x), the cornea is a middle-layer view (16x), and cells/flare in the…

q: On slit lamp exam in a dark room, the beam through the anterior chamber shows a hazy "headlight-in-fog" glow with no discrete particles. What does this indicate?
w: Flare is the Tyndall haze of leaked protein — a diffuse glow with no distinct particles. CELLS are discrete floating white blood cells that look like dust in a sunbeam. Flare = protein; cells = inflammatory cells. Both are assessed at high magnification (25–40x) in a fully dark room. This is separate from Van Herick, which grades the angle, not inflammation.
k: Flare is the Tyndall haze of leaked protein — a diffuse glow with no distinct particles.

q: During a JCC power check with the tentative cylinder at −0.75D × 090, the patient consistently prefers Position 1. Which adjustment is indicated?
o: Rotate the axis clockwise toward the red dot
w: Position 1 means red dots parallel to the tentative axis — this is the deliberate overcorrection position. If the patient prefers it, the cylinder is still under-powered relative to the optimum. Adding −0.25D steps (with +0.25D sphere compensation per −0.50D cyl added) moves toward equality. A is the response to a Position 2 preference. C describes the axis check technique, not the power check. D adjusts only the sphere without addressing the cylinder deficiency.
k: Position 1 means red dots parallel to the tentative axis — this is the deliberate overcorrection position.

q: During the prism dissociation binocular balance, the patient reports the upper image (OD) is consistently clearer. After adding +0.25D to the OD, the lower image (OS) becomes clearer instead. What is the correct next step?
o: Add another +0.25D to OD to see if equality appears
Allow OD to retain the better vision and begin binocular reduction
Add +0.25D to OS to bring it back
Repeat the monocular subjective refraction for OD before continuing
w: When adding +0.25D reverses rather than equalizes the preference, you have passed through the neutral point. The correct rule from this source: permit the dominant eye to retain the better vision and proceed. A would cause the same problem again (bouncing the preference back and forth). B would reverse the error to OS, not resolve it. D is not indicated — the monocular subjective was already completed; the issue is at the binocular balance step.
k: When adding +0.25D reverses rather than equalizes the preference, you have passed through the neutral point.

q: A 7-year-old presents with 20/20 OD and 20/60 OS. Examination shows 4 Δ esotropia OU with +3.50D hyperopia. Cycloplegic refraction confirms +3.50D OU. What is the most appropriate first management step?
o: Prescribe full cycloplegic correction and reassess
Prescribe BO prism to compensate for the esotropia
Patch the right eye for 6 hours/day
w: This is a classic accommodative esotropia presentation: esotropia with significant hyperopia. The uncorrected hyperopia drives accommodation, which drives convergence via the AC/A ratio, producing the esotropia. The first step is full hyperopic correction — the glasses may resolve the deviation completely. Patching is premature before the refractive error is addressed; the amblyopia in OS may partially or fully resolve with correction alone. Surgery is premature; surgery on an uncorrected accommodative esotropia treats the symptom without removing the cause. Prism would compensate the deviation without removing the accommodative drive; the esotropia would persist when the prism is removed.
k: This is a classic accommodative esotropia presentation: esotropia with significant hyperopia.

q: A patient has 8 Δ exophoria at near. BI recovery at near (#17B) is 10 Δ. The examiner wants to prescribe compensating prism. How much prism is needed according to Sheard's criterion, and in which direction?
o: No prism needed; criterion is met
w: Sheard's criterion: BI recovery (10 Δ) must be ≥ 2 × phoria (2 × 8 = 16 Δ). It is not met (10 < 16). Prism needed = (2 × 8 − 10) ÷ 3 = (16 − 10) ÷ 3 = 6 ÷ 3 = 2 Δ. Direction: exophoria → BI prism (base-in compensates for the outward deviation). C over-estimates the amount.
k: Sheard's criterion: BI recovery (10 Δ) must be ≥ 2 × phoria (2 × 8 = 16 Δ).

q: The avoidable share is:
w: Avoidable share: over 80%.

q: The PH leading cause of BLINDNESS is:
w: PH leading cause of BLINDNESS: cataract (~33–35%).
k: PH leading cause of BLINDNESS = cataract (~33–35%)

q: The PH leading cause of LOW VISION is:
w: PH leading cause of LOW VISION: refractive error (~43%).
k: PH leading cause of LOW VISION = refractive error (~43%)

q: The amblyopia among those is:
w: Amblyopia among those: ~25%.

q: The functional presbyopia threshold is:
w: Functional presbyopia threshold: AOA < 5.00 D. Near symptoms develop; ADD indicated

q: The absolute presbyopia AOA is:
w: Absolute presbyopia AOA: ~0.25D or less. Near 75 years old per Donders

q: The standard working distance (retinoscopy) is:
w: Standard working distance (retinoscopy): 67 cm → −1.50D correction. Some rooms use 50 cm → −2.00D

q: The myopia classification (medium) is:
w: Myopia classification (medium): 3.00 to 6.00 D.

q: The myopia classification (high) is:
w: Myopia classification (high): 6.00 D and above. Risk of degenerative changes

q: The hyperopia (medium) is:
w: Hyperopia (medium): 3.00 to 5.00 D.

q: The hyperopia (high) is:
w: Hyperopia (high): 5.00 D and above.

q: The donders table — age 20 is:
w: Donders table — age 20: 10.00 D. Key anchor: memorize age 20 = 10D

q: The donders table — age 40 is:
w: Donders table — age 40: 4.50 D. Beginning of presbyopia range

q: The donders table — age 45 is:
w: Donders table — age 45: 3.50 D. Typical clinical presbyopia onset

q: Which term describes parallel rays focus exactly on the retina at rest; no correction needed?
w: Emmetropia: Greek: emmetros (in proper measure). Parallel rays focus exactly on the retina at rest; no correction needed
k: Emmetropia = Parallel rays focus exactly on the retina at rest; no correction needed

q: Which term describes eye "squints to see" — near-sightedness; image in front of retina?
w: Myopia: Greek: myein (to close/shut) + ops (eye). Eye "squints to see" — near-sightedness; image in front of retina
k: Myopia = Eye "squints to see" — near-sightedness; image in front of retina

q: Which term describes "Over-seeing" — far-sightedness; image behind retina?
w: Hyperopia: Greek: hyper (over/beyond) + ops (eye). "Over-seeing" — far-sightedness; image behind retina

q: Which term describes same condition as hyperopia; British/older preferred term; both valid?
w: Hypermetropia: hyper + metron + ops. Same condition as hyperopia; British/older preferred term; both valid
k: Hypermetropia = Same condition as hyperopia; British/older preferred term; both valid

q: Which term describes "No point focus" — different powers in different meridians; multiple foci?
w: Astigmatism: Greek: a (not) + stigma (point). "No point focus" — different powers in different meridians; multiple foci
k: Astigmatism = "No point focus" — different powers in different meridians; multiple foci

q: Which term describes age-related loss of accommodative amplitude; lens stiffening?
w: Presbyopia: Greek: presbys (old man) + ops (eye). Age-related loss of accommodative amplitude; lens stiffening
k: Presbyopia = Age-related loss of accommodative amplitude; lens stiffening

q: Which term describes refractive error differs between the two eyes?
w: Anisometropia: Greek: aniso (unequal) + metron + ops. Refractive error differs between the two eyes
k: Anisometropia = Refractive error differs between the two eyes

q: Which term describes unequal image size between the two eyes?
w: Aniseikonia: Greek: aniso (unequal) + eikon (image). Unequal image size between the two eyes
k: Aniseikonia = Unequal image size between the two eyes

q: Which term describes absence of crystalline lens; extreme hyperopia results?
w: Aphakia: Greek: a (without) + phakos (lens). Absence of crystalline lens; extreme hyperopia results
k: Aphakia = Absence of crystalline lens; extreme hyperopia results

q: Which term describes amount of hyperopia that can be overcome by accommodation?
w: Facultative hyperopia: Latin: facultas (ability). Amount of hyperopia that can be overcome by accommodation
k: Facultative hyperopia = Amount of hyperopia that can be overcome by accommodation

q: Which term describes amount masked by tonic ciliary spasm; only revealed under cycloplegia?
w: Latent hyperopia: Latin: latere (to hide). Amount masked by tonic ciliary spasm; only revealed under cycloplegia
k: Latent hyperopia = Amount masked by tonic ciliary spasm; only revealed under cycloplegia

q: Which term describes amount measurable by routine refraction without cycloplegia?
w: Manifest hyperopia: Latin: manifestus (obvious). Amount measurable by routine refraction without cycloplegia
k: Manifest hyperopia = Amount measurable by routine refraction without cycloplegia

q: Which term describes prism diopters of convergence generated per diopter of accommodation?
w: AC/A ratio: Accommodative Convergence to Accommodation. Prism diopters of convergence generated per diopter of accommodation
k: AC/A ratio = Prism diopters of convergence generated per diopter of accommodation

q: Which term describes apparent myopia from tonic accommodative spasm in a non-myopic eye?
w: Pseudomyopia: Greek: pseudo (false). Apparent myopia from tonic accommodative spasm in a non-myopic eye
k: Pseudomyopia = Apparent myopia from tonic accommodative spasm in a non-myopic eye

q: The normal PD (children) is:
w: Normal PD (children): 41–55mm (50–60mm). Can be as low as 45mm

q: The normal pupil (room light) is:
w: Normal pupil (room light): 4.0mm (range 2.5–5.0mm).

q: The normal pupil (darkness) is:
w: Normal pupil (darkness): 6.5mm (range 5.0–8.5mm).

q: The normal pupil (direct light) is:
w: Normal pupil (direct light): 3.0mm (range 2.0–4.5mm).

q: The anisocoria threshold is:
w: Anisocoria threshold: >1.0mm difference = abnormal. Physiologic = same in light and dark

q: The angle kappa (normal) is:
w: Angle kappa (normal): Up to +5° (10Δ) nasally. Larger positive = simulates exo; temporal = simulates eso

q: Which term describes conjugate binocular eye movement — both eyes move same direction simultaneously?
w: Version: Latin: versio (turning). Conjugate binocular eye movement — both eyes move same direction simultaneously
k: Version = Conjugate binocular eye movement — both eyes move same direction simultaneously

q: Which term describes monocular eye movement in any direction?
w: Duction: Latin: ducere (to lead). Monocular eye movement in any direction
k: Duction = Monocular eye movement in any direction

q: Which term describes binocular eye movements in opposite directions (convergence/divergence)?
w: Vergence: Latin: vergere (to incline). Binocular eye movements in opposite directions (convergence/divergence)
k: Vergence = Binocular eye movements in opposite directions (convergence/divergence)

q: Which term describes closest point the eyes can maintain convergence without diplopia or deviation?
w: NPC: Near Point of Convergence. Closest point the eyes can maintain convergence without diplopia or deviation
k: NPC = Closest point the eyes can maintain convergence without diplopia or deviation

q: Which term describes closest point the eye can still focus clearly (maximum accommodation used)?
w: NPA: Near Point of Accommodation. Closest point the eye can still focus clearly (maximum accommodation used)
k: NPA = Closest point the eye can still focus clearly (maximum accommodation used)

q: Which term describes latent deviation — controlled by fusion; only appears when fusion is broken?
w: Phoria: Greek: phora (carrying). Latent deviation — controlled by fusion; only appears when fusion is broken
k: Phoria = Latent deviation — controlled by fusion; only appears when fusion is broken

q: Which term describes manifest deviation — constant misalignment even with both eyes open?
w: Tropia: Greek: tropos (turning). Manifest deviation — constant misalignment even with both eyes open
k: Tropia = Manifest deviation — constant misalignment even with both eyes open

q: Which term describes paradoxical dilation on swinging flashlight = afferent defect ipsilateral side?
w: RAPD: Relative Afferent Pupillary Defect. Paradoxical dilation on swinging flashlight = afferent defect ipsilateral side
k: RAPD = Paradoxical dilation on swinging flashlight = afferent defect ipsilateral side

q: Which term describes rAPD; impaired optic nerve or retinal input compared to fellow eye?
w: Marcus Gunn pupil: Named after Robert Marcus Gunn. RAPD; impaired optic nerve or retinal input compared to fellow eye
k: Marcus Gunn pupil = RAPD; impaired optic nerve or retinal input compared to fellow eye

q: Which term describes corneal reflex method to estimate tropia magnitude in prism diopters?
w: Hirschberg test: Named after Julius Hirschberg. Corneal reflex method to estimate tropia magnitude in prism diopters
k: Hirschberg test = Corneal reflex method to estimate tropia magnitude in prism diopters

q: Which term describes standard abbreviation for normal pupil findings?
w: PERRL: Pupils Equal Round Reactive to Light. Standard abbreviation for normal pupil findings
k: PERRL = Standard abbreviation for normal pupil findings

q: Which term describes unequal pupil sizes; physiologic if difference ≤1mm and consistent in light and dark?
w: Anisocoria: Greek: aniso (unequal) + kore (pupil). Unequal pupil sizes; physiologic if difference ≤1mm and consistent in light and dark
k: Anisocoria = Unequal pupil sizes; physiologic if difference ≤1mm and consistent in light and dark

q: The indirect ophthalmoscopy magnification is:
w: Indirect ophthalmoscopy magnification: 2–5x (monocular); 1.5–4.5x (binocular). Inverted, real image

q: The normal K reading range is:
w: Normal K reading range: 40–47D (average ~43–44D). Outside range: investigate

q: The normal corneal astigmatism is:
w: Normal corneal astigmatism: ≤1.00D (most common). Higher = clinically significant

q: The slit lamp eyepiece (ocular) lens power is:
w: Slit lamp eyepiece (ocular) lens power: +10 D. Fixed magnifier
k: Slit lamp eyepiece (ocular) lens power = +10 D

q: The slit lamp objective lens power is:
w: Slit lamp objective lens power: +22 D. Fixed magnifier
k: Slit lamp objective lens power = +22 D

q: Which term describes examination of the interior of the eye with an ophthalmoscope?
w: Ophthalmoscopy: Greek: ophthalmos (eye) + skopein. Examination of the interior of the eye with an ophthalmoscope
k: Ophthalmoscopy = Examination of the interior of the eye with an ophthalmoscope

q: Which term describes slit lamp beam width ~3mm; allows 3D view of corneal volume?
w: Parallelepiped: Geometry: parallel + solid. Slit lamp beam width ~3mm; allows 3D view of corneal volume
k: Parallelepiped = Slit lamp beam width ~3mm; allows 3D view of corneal volume

q: Which term describes slit lamp method to estimate anterior chamber angle depth?
w: Van Herick technique: Named after W. Van Herick. Slit lamp method to estimate anterior chamber angle depth
k: Van Herick technique = Slit lamp method to estimate anterior chamber angle depth

q: Which term describes the target circles or crosses reflected off the cornea in keratometry?
w: Mire: French: mirer (to aim at / reflect). The target circles or crosses reflected off the cornea in keratometry
k: Mire = The target circles or crosses reflected off the cornea in keratometry

q: Which term describes formula to predict total astigmatism from corneal astigmatism?
w: Javal's rule: Named after Emile Javal. Formula to predict total astigmatism from corneal astigmatism
k: Javal's rule = Formula to predict total astigmatism from corneal astigmatism

q: The ideal fog lens is:
w: Ideal fog lens: +0.50 to +0.75D above best sphere. Standardizes the accommodative relaxation before astigmatic dial
k: Ideal fog lens = +0.50 to +0.75D above best sphere

q: The JCC strengths is:
w: JCC strengths: ±0.12, ±0.25, ±0.50D. ±0.25 is the standard clinical choice

q: The sphere add per cylinder is:
w: Sphere add per cylinder: +0.25D sphere per −0.50D cyl. Keeps the circle of least confusion centered on the retina
k: Sphere add per cylinder = +0.25D sphere per −0.50D cyl

q: The binocular balance re-fog is:
w: Binocular balance re-fog: +0.50 to +0.75D to both eyes. Same principle as monocular fog; applied binocularly
k: Binocular balance re-fog = +0.50 to +0.75D to both eyes

q: The NRA normal is:
w: NRA normal: +2.00D. Ability to relax accommodation at 40cm

q: The PRA normal is:
w: PRA normal: −2.37D. Ability to stimulate accommodation at 40cm

q: Which term describes deliberate over-plussing to blur the image and relax accommodation?
w: Fogging: English: to cloud or obscure. Deliberate over-plussing to blur the image and relax accommodation
k: Fogging = Deliberate over-plussing to blur the image and relax accommodation

q: Which term describes reducing plus (or adding minus) after astigmatic correction is set?
w: Defogging: Removing the fog. Reducing plus (or adding minus) after astigmatic correction is set
k: Defogging = Reducing plus (or adding minus) after astigmatic correction is set

q: Which term describes the endpoint: highest plus (lowest minus) that still gives best VA?
w: MPMVA: Most Plus / Minimum Minus Visual Acuity. The endpoint: highest plus (lowest minus) that still gives best VA
k: MPMVA = The endpoint: highest plus (lowest minus) that still gives best VA

q: Which term describes two-color test based on chromatic aberration; bichrome = binocular version?
w: Duochrome / Bichrome: Greek duo (two) + chroma (color). Two-color test based on chromatic aberration; bichrome = binocular version
k: Duochrome / Bichrome = Two-color test based on chromatic aberration; bichrome = binocular version

q: Which term describes binocular balance technique using a septum to separate each eye's field?
w: TIB: Turville Infinity Balance. Binocular balance technique using a septum to separate each eye's field
k: TIB = Binocular balance technique using a septum to separate each eye's field

q: Which term describes the 21-step phorometric protocol that maps accommodation and convergence?
w: OEP: Optometric Extension Program. The 21-step phorometric protocol that maps accommodation and convergence
k: OEP = The 21-step phorometric protocol that maps accommodation and convergence

q: Which term describes maximum accommodation that can be stimulated at 40cm (#20)?
w: PRA: Positive Relative Accommodation. Maximum accommodation that can be stimulated at 40cm (#20)
k: PRA = Maximum accommodation that can be stimulated at 40cm (#20)

q: Which term describes maximum accommodation that can be inhibited at 40cm (#21)?
w: NRA: Negative Relative Accommodation. Maximum accommodation that can be inhibited at 40cm (#21)
k: NRA = Maximum accommodation that can be inhibited at 40cm (#21)

q: Which term describes manifest misalignment of the eyes; one eye deviates from the point of fixation?
w: Strabismus: Greek strabizein (to squint). Manifest misalignment of the eyes; one eye deviates from the point of fixation
k: Strabismus = Manifest misalignment of the eyes; one eye deviates from the point of fixation

q: Which term describes latent tendency of the eyes to deviate, held in check by fusion?
w: Phoria: Greek pherein (to carry). Latent tendency of the eyes to deviate, held in check by fusion
k: Phoria = Latent tendency of the eyes to deviate, held in check by fusion

q: Which term describes manifest deviation; the eye actually turns, not just tends to?
w: Tropia: Greek tropos (turning). Manifest deviation; the eye actually turns, not just tends to
k: Tropia = Manifest deviation; the eye actually turns, not just tends to

q: Which term describes fixation in the amblyopic eye occurs at a retinal point other than the fovea?
w: Eccentric fixation: Latin ex-centrum (off-center). Fixation in the amblyopic eye occurs at a retinal point other than the fovea
k: Eccentric fixation = Fixation in the amblyopic eye occurs at a retinal point other than the fovea

q: Which term describes deliberate blurring or occluding of the better eye to force use of the amblyopic eye?
w: Penalization: Latin poena (penalty). Deliberate blurring or occluding of the better eye to force use of the amblyopic eye
k: Penalization = Deliberate blurring or occluding of the better eye to force use of the amblyopic eye

q: Which term describes technique using intense light stimulation of the non-foveal area to rehabilitate foveal fixation in amblyopia with eccentric fixation?
w: Pleoptics: Greek pleion (more) + ops (eye/vision). Technique using intense light stimulation of the non-foveal area to rehabilitate foveal fixation in amblyopia with eccentric fixation
k: Pleoptics = Technique using intense light stimulation of the non-foveal area to rehabilitate foveal fixation i

q: Which term describes unequal image size between the two eyes, causing binocular fusion difficulties?
w: Aniseikonia: Greek anisos + eikon (image). Unequal image size between the two eyes, causing binocular fusion difficulties
k: Aniseikonia = Unequal image size between the two eyes, causing binocular fusion difficulties

q: Which term describes a lens designed to equalize retinal image size without altering its power?
w: Iseikonic lens: Greek isos (equal) + eikon. Lens designed to equalize retinal image size without altering optical power
k: Iseikonic lens = Lens designed to equalize retinal image size without altering optical power

q: Which term describes broader term; includes accommodation, sensory fusion, and higher perceptual training — not just convergence?
w: Vision therapy (VT): English compound. Broader term; includes accommodation, sensory fusion, and higher perceptual training — not just convergence
k: Vision therapy (VT) = Broader term; includes accommodation, sensory fusion, and higher perceptual training — n

q: Which term describes monocular technique for eccentric fixation; uses intense light to dazzle peripheral retina and rehabilitate foveal fixation?
w: Pleoptics: Greek pleion (more) + ops. Monocular technique for eccentric fixation; uses intense light to dazzle peripheral retina and rehabilitate foveal fixation
k: Pleoptics = Monocular technique for eccentric fixation; uses intense light to dazzle peripheral retina and reh

q: Which term describes fixation in the amblyopic eye at a retinal point other than the fovea; marker of deep amblyopia?
w: Eccentric fixation: Latin ex-centrum (off-center). Fixation in the amblyopic eye at a retinal point other than the fovea; marker of deep amblyopia
k: Eccentric fixation = Fixation in the amblyopic eye at a retinal point other than the fovea; marker of deep amb

q: Which term describes normal doubling of non-fixated objects; expected finding when fusion is intact; absent when suppression is present?
w: Physiological diplopia: Greek diploos (double). Normal doubling of non-fixated objects; expected finding when fusion is intact; absent when suppression is present
k: Physiological diplopia = Normal doubling of non-fixated objects; expected finding when fusion is intact; absen

q: Which term describes step 3 of Parks test; tilting the head increases vertical fusion demand on cyclovertical muscles of the ipsilateral eye?
w: Bielschowsky head tilt test: Named: Alfred Bielschowsky (1900s). Step 3 of Parks test; tilting the head increases vertical fusion demand on cyclovertical muscles of the ipsilateral eye
k: Bielschowsky head tilt test = Step 3 of Parks test; tilting the head increases vertical fusion demand on cyclo

q: Which term describes instrument for measuring objective and subjective angle of deviation and training at all three Grades of BV?
w: Synoptophore / Amblyoscope: Greek synoptos (seen together). Instrument for measuring objective and subjective angle of deviation and training at all three Grades of BV
k: Synoptophore / Amblyoscope = Instrument for measuring objective and subjective angle of deviation and training

q: Which term describes rotating high-contrast gratings; spatially stimulates amblyopic eye monocularly; 7 min/session?
w: CAM vision stimulator: Cambridge (Univ.) Amblyopia Model. Rotating high-contrast gratings; spatially stimulates amblyopic eye monocularly; 7 min/session
k: CAM vision stimulator = Rotating high-contrast gratings; spatially stimulates amblyopic eye monocularly; 7 min

q: Which term describes presents different images to each eye simultaneously; forces cortical integration; modern active amblyopia therapy?
w: Dichoptic training: Greek dikha (separately) + optikos. Presents different images to each eye simultaneously; forces cortical integration; modern active amblyopia therapy
k: Dichoptic training = Presents different images to each eye simultaneously; forces cortical integration; modern

q: In OEP analysis, which refractive condition BUFFERS the accommodative pattern?
w: OEP pairs each pattern with the error that cushions it: hyperopia buffers the accommodative pattern, exophoria buffers the convergence pattern. Recognising the buffer tells you which pattern the case is really about.
k: Hyperopia buffers accommodation; exophoria buffers convergence.

q: In OEP analysis, which condition BUFFERS the convergence pattern?
w: Exophoria buffers convergence, hyperopia buffers accommodation. The convergence pattern is the mechanism for maintaining single vision, so the phoria that relieves convergence demand is the buffer.

q: On OEP probe testing, a CONVEX lens inhibits accommodation and makes the target appear:
w: Plus inhibits accommodation: the object seems to move AWAY and appears BIGGER. Minus stimulates accommodation: the object seems to come CLOSER and appears SMALLER. Base-in behaves like plus, base-out like minus.
k: Plus/BI: away + bigger. Minus/BO: closer + smaller.

q: On OEP probe testing, BASE-OUT prism stimulates convergence and makes the target appear:
w: Base-out stimulates convergence, so the object seems to approach and shrink, mirroring a minus lens. Base-in inhibits convergence and mirrors a plus lens: away and bigger.
k: BO = closer + smaller (like minus); BI = away + bigger (like plus).

q: OEP test #3 is performed at 20 feet with which lenses in place?
w: #3 uses the habitual correction and measures lateral imbalance at far as the patient currently lives it. #8 repeats the measurement at far but through the test #7 lenses, so the pair shows what the new prescription changed.
